turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer has our opinion in case 16-424, Class v. United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Rodney Class, who is the petitioner, was charged with unlawfully possessing firearms in a locked jeep that was parked on the grounds of the US Capitol right here in Washington. He first challenged his indictment on the ground that he said the statute violates the Constitution, the Second Amendment and the Due Process Clause. The District Court disagreed, dismissed his claims. He then pleaded guilty and he entered into a written plea agreement where he agreed to waive certain appeals and certain rights, but that agreement said nothing about his challenge to the constitutionality of the statute, whether he could do that on direct appeal, and just was silent. Now, he went to the Court of Appeals and they said we are not going to listen to your argument because the guilty plea itself, by entering into a plea of guilty, you waived your right to claim that the statute was unconstitutional. And that is the question before us, does a guilty plea by itself bar the defendant's claim that the statute under which he was convicted is unconstitutional? We hold that it does not bar an appeal on that claim. Now, we think our holding flows pretty directly from precedent. 50 years ago, in Haynes v. United States, this Court had a very similar question before it and Justice Harlan in writing for the Court said "a plea of guilty does not of course waive his previous constitutional claim." Now, he does not go into that in detail, but the decision there reflects a history roughly dating back more than a century that roughly would justify that statement. And then in subsequent decisions this Court elaborated upon the statement. In a case called Blackledge v. Perry and Menna v. New York, both of those cases we basically held that while a guilty plea itself does bar challenges to certain case related antecedent constitutional violations, one say that stem from grand jury violations and certain other things, a guilty plea does not by itself bar a claim challenging the very power of the state to criminalize the defendant's conduct, the conduct to which he admitted. And we have since reaffirmed the Menna-Blackledge Doctrine's basic teaching that a plea of guilty to a charge does not waive a claim that judged on its face the charge is one which the state may not constitutionally prosecute. So as we understand them, the constitutional claims here at issue do not contradict the indictment or the plea agreement, they contradict the statute. They say the statute under which this thing was brought is no good and they can be resolved, we think, as far as we understand them or based on the existing record. In these circumstances we hold that a guilty plea does not bar a direct appeal. The government and the dissent point to Rule 11(a)(2) of the Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure, which sets out a system for entering into a conditional guilty plea. I plead guilty on the condition that this evidence is found to have been lawfully admitted or something like that. But that rule does not say whether it sets forth the exclusive procedure for bringing up this kind of claim, a constitutional challenge to the statute following a guilty plea. And the drafter said rather specifically that our rule, Rule 11(a)(2), has no application to the kinds of constitutional objections that might be raised under the Menna-Blackledge Doctrine and that is the doctrine which applies here. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we hold a guilty plea does not by itself bar a defendant's challenge to the constitutionality of the statute of conviction on a direct appeal. So we reverse the decision below and we remand for further proceedings. Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion, which Justice Kennedy and Justice Thomas have joined.
